channel.title: abcodex · release log
channel.description: What is new in abcodex. Daily ingests, prompted batches, depth passes.

item.title: Game over-exposure is a myth, and engineering ownership starts at problem definition
item.description: Two new cards. One theme in two domains: the constraint you think is real often isn't, and the work starts earlier than you think.

item.title: Adversarial agents rewrote a million lines in 11 days, AI worldview maps to alignment not geography, and tech workers split personal optimism from institutional trust
item.description: Three new cards, one new operator profile. Two themes: agentic workflows at production scale, and how AI reshapes how workers assess the industry they work in.

item.title: AI baseline creep drives burnout, overclaiming poisons adoption, and product builders hold four beliefs that collectively stall distribution investment
item.description: Four new cards, two new operator profiles. The day's scan covered AI's actual impact on workers and organizations, a cognitive trap that keeps product builders building the wrong thing, and a structural shift in what creative work demands when execution becomes free.

item.title: AI Overviews decouple citation from recommendation, benchmark-shaped content earns 3.3x more AI citations, and frontier models self-route tasks to cheaper subagents
item.description: Four new cards across two themes: how AI Overviews now separate citation from recommendation in ways that can backfire on self-promotional content, and how AI agents in agentic workflows perform best when given judgment about task routing rather than pre-written routing rules.

item.title: Harness-specific model drift, cross-model review practice, concurrent agent norms at OpenAI, and the SaaS category rotation
item.description: Four new cards across two themes: how model capability improvements can mask tool reliability regressions in specific harnesses, and how AI agent adoption is normalizing concurrent multi-agent workflows while restructuring SaaS market performance by category.

item.title: Agent routing before model choice, schema gaps causing retry loops, and AI search fragmentation across three non-overlapping engines
item.description: Four new cards, one new operator. Two cards on agent infrastructure decisions that determine outcome before model selection happens. One card on why developers need conceptual fluency to direct agent work. One card on AI search visibility as three separate non-overlapping platforms.

item.title: Product design before evals, hidden tokenizer costs, and exponential pace mismatches
item.description: Four new cards, one new operator. Two cards on signals teams miss until scale forces the issue (eval difficulty as a product smell, tokenizer inflation as a hidden price increase). Two cards on what happens when fast-moving systems outpace the structures meant to govern them.

item.title: Domain expertise as the agentic multiplier, long-horizon run economics, and the Sicko theory of Steam demos
item.description: Four new cards from June 30 and June 16, 2026. Two Mollick cards from his June 30 essay on the post-chatbot era. One Anthropic research card from Hitzig et al. showing cross-occupational coding parity. One Zukowski card on demo conversion mechanics.

item.title: AI attribution shifts, compute cost inversions, and agentic diffusion beyond engineering
item.description: Three new cards from June 29-30, 2026. AI brand mentions drive direct traffic lift. AI-native cost structures invert traditional SaaS ratios. Agentic tools spread into legal and research when skills-sharing lowers the adoption floor.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-29
item.description: Two new cards, two new operators, no new patterns. A thin but honest scan. Three prior daily runs captured most of the June 22-28 window. What remained: Joe Hudson on emotional clarity as the scarce advantage in the AI era, and Jason Cohen on why RICE-style confidence scoring is structurally broken.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-28
item.description: Three new cards, no new operators, no new patterns. Two themes from June 22-25. AI positioning built on substitution messaging erodes the trust of the workers it needs. And at the infrastructure level, agent harness loops are producing a quality problem as they scale, while the architecture that contains them is converging independently at thousands of shops.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-27
item.description: Three new cards, no new operators, no new patterns. Two themes from June 22-27. AI inference economics splits by latency tier and moat timing. Prompt injection gets a structural explanation that reframes why text-level defenses keep failing.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-26
item.description: Three new cards, one new operator, no new patterns. Two themes from the week of June 22-26. AI infrastructure pricing splits between commoditized rails and outcome-based software. A Valve algorithm change quietly reshuffled indie game pre-launch discovery.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-18
item.description: Four new cards, two new operators, no new patterns. Three themes from the first three weeks of June. Model savings get recycled into more tokens. Organizational structure sets the AI ceiling. Fast feedback is the discipline, not a tactic.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-17
item.description: Three new cards, one new operator, one new synthesis pattern. Two themes from the week of June 10-16. The model layer has commoditized and the moat is now operational. The ceiling on what AI does in production is set by policy, not silicon.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-16
item.description: Three new cards, three new operators. Knowledge work's sandwich structure explains why AI volume hasn't become job loss. The economics of token spend split into compile-once versus run-forever. And frontier AI capability is now gated by policy, not hardware.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-15
item.description: Three new cards, three new operators. AI coding agents are becoming user-acquisition channels for developer platforms. Apple Silicon clusters now run trillion-parameter models locally via Thunderbolt 5. B2B buyers research vendors in AI tools before any sales contact.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-14
item.description: Four new cards, two new operators. Frontier models capable of hours-long autonomous runs are reshaping how practitioners delegate work. Apple's 2026 developer platform introduces agents as a first-class primitive across Siri and Xcode.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-13
item.description: Three new cards, no new operators. April Dunford argues that AI market uncertainty has made vendor point of view the missing ingredient in enterprise sales. Kevin Indig publishes data showing algorithm-gifted search visibility reverts while authority-backed gains hold. Simon Willison offers a practical proxy for comparing model training depth.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-12
item.description: Five new cards, no new operators. Boris Cherny's Fortune interview series (June 8-11) reveals how cascading agent architecture works in production, how to benchmark AI ROI against labor rather than software, and what happens to code review when code writing is solved. Simon Willison documents frontier agent breadth-first problem decomposition. Rand Fishkin publishes 2026 data showing zero-click search has crossed 68% and explains why it will not reverse.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-11
item.description: Four new insight cards, no new operators. Two themes emerged from this week's operator output: frontier AI shifting the human role from active steering to commissioning and judging, and the production-system implications of routing and transparency choices.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-09
item.description: Three new insight cards, one new operator. Two themes emerged from this week's operator output: making AI adoption organizationally sustainable, and how Google's May 2026 Core Update reset visibility around source type fit rather than authority alone.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-06-04
item.description: Five new insight cards, one new operator. Two themes from the AI engineering lane and one from future-of-work converged this week on the economics and safety of running agents in production.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-05-27
item.description: Five new insight cards, two new operators, one new synthesis pattern. Three independent specialists named structural failures in their respective lanes within 48 hours of each other.

item.title: What landed today, 2026-05-25
item.description: Four new insights, zero new operators, three pattern updates. The AEO citation universe split into two distinct populations. Verification remained the rate limiter across every domain AI accelerated.

item.title: Five insights, two themes. AI search rewired at the platform level. VOC craft is the answer.
item.description: Five new cards across five operators. Two themes: AI search architecture changed at the platform level (Mike King, Rand Fishkin, Barry Schwartz), and content that resonates is built from verbatim buyer language not synthesis (Maja Voje, Nicolas Cole).

item.title: One level down: two operators on why surface-level matching misses what matters
item.description: Two new insights, two synthesis patterns updated, one new operator. One meta-theme ran through both: surface-level matching fails where depth is needed. Shreyas Doshi names it for strategy. Anthropic names it for security.

item.title: AEO splits into three jobs, the judgment ceiling holds, and listening compounds
item.description: Nine new cards. AEO fractures into citation, traffic, and IP protection as three separate jobs. The judgment ceiling holds as execution costs drop. One strong data set on customer marketing as systematic listening.

item.title: Five new insights: recovery floors, AI consensus, and task-driven minimalism
item.description: Five new cards from five operators. Rewind names the chilling effect that recovery gaps create on AI adoption. Packy McCormick and Nicolas Cole converge on distinctiveness as the post-AI scarcity from independent angles. Pawel Huryn and Chris Orlob arrive at the same minimalist discipline applied to skill acquisition and outreach sequencing.

item.title: Earn the citation, hold the floor, retire the position metric
item.description: Four new cards from Lily Ray, Aleyda Solis, Eli Schwartz, and Aakash Gupta. Ray and Solis converge on the structural architecture of AEO visibility from opposite directions. Schwartz names the measurement construct that is dissolving beneath the whole category. Gupta gives a one-sentence test that changes how you scope a skills library.
